title: Detailed Design front-matter
description: Front-matter shape for a Detailed Design artifact. Lives at /specs/{leaf-scope}/detailed_design.md — leaf scopes only; branch scopes carry Architecture instead. Composes the universal envelope and adds artifact-level identity (scope, parent_scope, parent_architecture, derived_from, governing_adrs). Status uses the universal lifecycle vocabulary (draft | active | superseded). The seven mandatory body sections (Metadata, Overview, Public Interface, Data Structures, Algorithms, State, Error Handling per TARGET §5.3) live in prose / Mermaid / code fences inside the markdown body — this schema validates only the front-matter YAML. Per-entry embedded-YAML shapes are exported as $defs/public_interface_entry, $defs/data_structure_entry, and $defs/error_matrix_row for parsers and validators that extract individual blocks. The public-interface def lifts the Design-by-Contract discipline from architecture.schema.json's $defs/interface (preconditions / postconditions.on_success|on_failure / typed errors) but makes the sub-fields optional: rigor is a Quality Bar concern, shape is schema's. Recovery_status narrows map-form 'overview' to verified | unknown only — the Overview section carries intent (why this leaf exists, what invariants must hold), which is a human-only field under the no-fabrication rule. Scalar recovery_status keeps the full 3-value vocabulary because DD's dominant content (interfaces, data, algorithms, state, error matrix) is code-observable, so a whole-artifact 'reconstructed' is legitimate for the structural side even when intent must be re-verified separately.

id.description: Artifact identifier. Pattern DD-{scope-flattened}-{name}, where the scope's '/' separators are flattened to '-' and the leaf-local name follows. Examples: scope 'app/jobs' with name 'dequeue-service' → 'DD-app-jobs-dequeue-service'; scope 'legacy/auth' with name 'session-validator' → 'DD-legacy-session-validator'. The schema does not enforce a structural scope/name split — it only requires the DD- prefix followed by kebab-case segments — so team conventions on the leaf-name portion remain flexible.

artifact_type.description: Artifact-type discriminator, fixed for this schema.

scope.description: Scope path this Detailed Design covers. Kebab-case segments separated by '/'. Empty string denotes root scope — only legal for single-module systems where the root is itself a leaf.

parent_scope.description: Parent scope path. Null only when this DD lives at the root (rare — single-module systems). For the normal leaf case, parent_scope is the containing branch's scope.

parent_architecture.description: The Architecture artifact that allocates this leaf's responsibilities. Required — every leaf DD is governed by a parent Architecture's Decomposition entry. Tightened to the ARCH id pattern so broken references surface at schema validation rather than deferring to the traceability tool.

derived_from.description: Upstream artifact ids this DD is refined from. Typically at least one requirement (REQ-…) plus optionally sibling DDs, ARCH interfaces, or ADRs that shape the contract. Non-empty list; empty would mean an orphan design.

governing_adrs.description: Cross-cutting ADRs that constrain this leaf. Optional; empty or absent when the leaf inherits no ADR-level constraints beyond what its Architecture already references. Tightened to the ADR id pattern — the reference target is unambiguous by context.

status.description: Lifecycle state. DD uses the universal lifecycle vocabulary: draft → active, re-enters draft on material edit, transitions to superseded only when a differently-named DD document replaces this one (rare; routine revisions bump version instead).

recovery_status.description: Retrofit provenance. Scalar form applies the status to the whole artifact — 'reconstructed' is legitimate here because DD's dominant content (interfaces, data structures, algorithms, state, error matrix) is code-observable. Map form targets individual sections / fields; the 'overview' key is narrowed to 'verified' | 'unknown' only because Overview carries intent (why this leaf exists, what invariants must hold), which is a human-only field under the no-fabrication rule. Other map keys accept the full 3-value vocabulary.

overview.description: Recovery status for the Overview section specifically. Human-only (intent, invariants) — the 'reconstructed' value is banned by the no-fabrication rule. Allowed: 'verified' or 'unknown'.

public_interface_entry.description: Per-function / per-method block shape — the YAML an embedded Public Interface entry in the markdown body renders to. Exported for parsers and validators extracting individual function contracts from a DD document. Not enforced by the top-level schema on the front-matter object itself. Lifts the Design-by-Contract discipline from the Architecture interface def (preconditions, postconditions split by outcome, typed errors, invariants) but makes sub-fields optional — shape vs completeness is the deliberate split: the schema refuses malformed contracts, the Quality Bar asserts that populated contracts are complete. The errors shape is lighter than Architecture's (no kebab-code or HTTP status) because DD errors describe internal raise/return semantics, not wire protocol.

name.description: Function / method name. Non-empty string; no stricter pattern so that team conventions (camelCase claimJob, snake_case claim_job, PascalCase ClaimJob) all fit.

signature.description: Language-neutral signature. Typical forms: 'claimJob(worker_id: string, lease_duration: Duration) -> ClaimResult' or 'fn claim_job(worker_id: &str, lease: Duration) -> Result<ClaimResult, ClaimError>'. Free-form — the Quality Bar asserts types are declared; the schema only asserts non-emptiness.

description.description: One- or two-sentence description of what this function does. Optional at schema level; the Quality Bar asserts presence on every public entry.

preconditions.description: What the caller must ensure on the way in. Each item is one precondition expressed as a predicate (value ranges, state conditions, relationship constraints, units). A function with no preconditions beyond 'callable' should state that explicitly — absence is a Quality Bar issue, not a schema violation.

postconditions.description: What the function guarantees on the way out. Split by outcome: on_success describes the normal return; on_failure describes the guaranteed state / raised error / partial-mutation guarantees when the function fails. The DbC value is that callers can reason about state after failure, not only after success; the split is what makes it work.

on_success.description: Guaranteed postconditions when the function returns normally. Typical content: return-value constraints, state mutation performed, idempotency window, old-value relationships.

on_failure.description: Guaranteed postconditions when the function fails. Typical content: 'no state mutation', 'partial mutation bounded to X', 'caller receives error Y'. An interface that does not declare on_failure postconditions is silently undefined on the failure path — a Quality Bar issue rather than a schema one.

invariants.description: Properties that hold before and after every public call. Typical examples: 'does not mutate its input parameters', 'does not acquire locks beyond the scope of this call', 'preserves monotonic ordering of the input sequence'. Optional at schema level.

errors.description: Error enumeration. Each entry names the error class / code the caller may observe and optionally describes when it is raised and what it means. Lighter shape than the Architecture interface errors (no HTTP status, no kebab-code pattern) because DD errors describe internal raise/return semantics, not wire protocol. Populated per the error_matrix cross-reference — rows in the Error Handling matrix should have a corresponding entry here for every error that crosses the public interface.

error.description: Error class, exception type, sentinel name, or result variant — whatever the caller inspects to distinguish outcomes. Free-form; team language conventions decide capitalization.

raised_when.description: Trigger condition — the precondition violation, state, or downstream failure that causes this error. Optional; absent when the 'error' name alone is self-describing.

meaning.description: One-line human-readable meaning — read by integrators, on-call engineers, and anyone writing error-handling logic.

side_effects.description: Observable side effects — I/O, shared-state mutation, observable reads, logging at non-debug levels, metrics emission. Required category per the HTML; absence is a Quality Bar issue.

thread_safety.description: Thread-safety guarantee the function commits to. The 'thread-hostile' category is explicit — some functions are safe only in single-threaded contexts, and silent assumptions are bugs.

nullability.description: Per-parameter / return nullability declarations. Each item names a parameter or 'return' and states whether null is permitted and what it means. Optional at schema level; Quality Bar asserts presence for every nullable parameter and return.

complexity_notes.description: Contractual time / space bounds when complexity is part of the contract (not a curiosity). Example: 'O(log n) in the number of active claims' or 'constant memory regardless of input size'. Optional.

data_structure_entry.description: Per-internal-data-structure block shape — the YAML an embedded Data Structures entry renders to. Exported for parsers and validators. Minimum committable shape: name + fields (name / type / invariant). Ownership, lifetime, and returned-object semantics are recorded when the data structure is observable across module boundaries; they are optional at schema level and asserted by the Quality Bar when the structure is publicly returned or long-lived.

name.description: Data structure name. PascalCase is typical (Claim, DequeueResult, SessionToken) but the schema does not enforce casing so that team conventions fit.

description.description: One- or two-sentence description of what this structure represents and where it is used. Optional; Quality Bar asserts presence.

fields.description: Per-field shape: name, type, invariant. 'type' is at one level of abstraction above the implementation language — 'integer', 'timestamp-utc', 'job-id' — so the same DD applies across language implementations. 'invariant' is the per-field property that holds whenever the structure is observed (non-negative, monotonic, matches foreign key, etc.); optional when the type alone captures the constraint.

invariant.description: Per-field invariant — what holds whenever this field is read. Optional when the type alone captures the constraint.

ownership.description: Who constructs, mutates, and releases instances of this structure. Examples: 'constructed by DequeueService.claimJob; read-only elsewhere', 'caller-owned; cleaned up at request scope exit', 'shared immutable — safe to alias'. Optional; required by Quality Bar for any structure that crosses a module boundary.

lifetime.description: How long instances live and what bounds their lifetime. Examples: 'request-scoped', 'per-connection', 'process lifetime (singleton)', 'event-sourced; persists in the outbox until drained'. Optional.

returned_semantics.description: When this structure is returned across the public interface: does the caller receive a copy, a live view, a read-only reference, or ownership? Explicit declarations prevent the subtle bug class where callers mutate what they believe is a copy. Optional when the structure is never returned.

error_matrix_row.description: Per-row shape of the Error Handling matrix. Exported for parsers and validators extracting individual rows. The five-column matrix (error / detection / containment / recovery / caller_receives) is the authoritative structure for DD error handling per the HTML page. Rows correspond directly to robustness test cases in the sibling TestSpec — the TestSpec's fault-injection and error cases trace back to these rows. 'recovery' is an enum because the five strategies (fail-fast / retry / fallback / compensate / propagate) are the stable vocabulary called out in the HTML best practices — silent strategies are a recognised anti-pattern.

error.description: Error condition — typically the exception class, error code, or sentinel value. Matches an entry in the corresponding public_interface_entry.errors array when this error crosses the public interface.

detection.description: How this error is detected — precondition check, downstream status, timeout, invariant violation, sensor reading. Specific enough that a test can force the condition.

containment.description: Where this error is handled — which function, layer, or boundary catches it. Optional; absent when the strategy is 'propagate' and containment is delegated upward by design.

recovery.description: Recovery strategy. Closed enum: fail-fast (abort the operation and surface the error to the caller), retry (with bound), fallback (alternate path), compensate (undo partial effects), propagate (re-raise / return to caller). Silence is not an option; every row declares a strategy.

caller_receives.description: What the caller observes when this error path fires. Exception type, return value, sentinel, error code, or structured result variant. Read by integrators writing error-handling logic; also the cross-check against the public_interface_entry.errors entry.
